title: Testing AI and ML Systems
summary: How to test AI and ML systems — quality characteristics, data quality, fairness, adversarial robustness, and the LLM-era extensions of the ISTQB® CT-AI syllabus. About AI as the system under test. Helps prepare for the ISTQB® CT-AI exam; not an official ISTQB® study guide and not affiliated with or endorsed by ISTQB®. ISTQB® is a registered trademark of the International Software Testing Qualifications Board.

volatileChapters.reason: LLM-system testing tooling (Promptfoo, DeepEval, Inspect, MCP-aware evals) shifts on a near-quarterly cadence.

inspiredBy.title: Introduction to AI Testing (aligned with ISTQB CT-AI v1.0 syllabus)

chapters.title: Introduction to AI
chapters.summary: AI systems are not classical software — they are shaped by data more than by code, and that distinction changes everything about how you test them.

chapters.title: Quality Characteristics for AI-Based Systems
chapters.summary: AI systems have quality characteristics that classical software doesn't — flexibility, autonomy, evolution, bias, ethics, side-effects — and the testing strategy is shaped by which of these matter for the system under test.

chapters.title: Machine Learning — A Tester's Perspective
chapters.summary: The ML lifecycle is a sequence of testable artifacts — data, features, model, deployment, monitoring — and a tester's job is to know which controls operate at each stage and where the leverage is.

chapters.title: ML Data: The Real Test Surface
chapters.summary: In ML systems, data IS the test surface — quality, distribution, balance, privacy, leakage, and labels are where most production bugs originate, and where the tester has the most leverage.

chapters.title: ML Functional Performance Metrics
chapters.summary: Accuracy is the metric that lies most often — and choosing the right metric for the system, knowing what each obscures, and reading a confusion matrix correctly are core skills for any tester signing off on an AI release.

chapters.title: ML — Bias, Fairness, and Explainability
chapters.summary: Bias, fairness, and explainability are the three quality characteristics that turn AI testing from a technical discipline into a regulated one — and the metrics, tools, and tradeoffs are different enough from classical metrics that testers need a separate toolkit.

chapters.title: ML Frameworks and Test Tooling
chapters.summary: The 2026 ML test toolkit divides into four categories — data validation, model validation, fairness/explainability, and end-to-end behavior testing — and a tester's literacy with the dominant tool in each category is what makes test plans concrete instead of abstract.

chapters.title: Testing AI-Based Systems Overview
chapters.summary: Testing an AI system is testing a pipeline — data, model, integration, deployment — not just a function with inputs and outputs, and the test pyramid for ML differs in shape and structure from the classical one.

chapters.title: Black-Box Testing of AI Systems
chapters.summary: Black-box techniques for AI — metamorphic testing, A/B testing, pairwise testing, back-to-back / differential testing — let you exercise a model without ground truth, by relying on relations between outputs instead of fixed expected values.

chapters.title: Adversarial Testing and Data Poisoning
chapters.summary: AI systems face attack vectors classical software does not — adversarial inputs designed to fool the model, poisoning attacks that contaminate training data, evasion at inference time, and model extraction — and a tester's job includes simulating each before an attacker does.

chapters.title: Testing Neural Networks Specifically
chapters.summary: Neural networks have their own test surface — coverage criteria over neurons and activations, surprise-based input adequacy measures, and a body of research that mostly demonstrates how brittle traditional coverage thinking becomes when the system has billions of parameters.

chapters.title: Testing LLM-Based Systems
chapters.summary: LLM-based systems are AI systems whose test surface goes beyond what the original CT-AI v1.0 syllabus covers — hallucination, prompt injection, jailbreaks, RAG evaluation, and a 2026 generation of eval frameworks that exist only because the older techniques do not catch what LLMs get wrong.
